---
title: "ExaVerif"
subtitle: "Exhaustive Verification for RISC‑V Custom Instructions"
version-prefix: 1
version-mark: true
format:
  pdf:
    classoption:
      - twocolumn
    mainfont: texgyreheros-regular.otf
    fontsize: 10pt
    mainfontoptions:
      - BoldFont=texgyreheros-bold.otf
      - ItalicFont=texgyreheros-italic.otf
      - BoldItalicFont=texgyreheros-bolditalic.otf
    geometry:
        - margin=14mm
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{xcolor}
        \usepackage{microtype}
        \setkeys{Gin}{width=\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio}
        \addtokomafont{disposition}{\rmfamily}
        \input{./_files/_metadata.tex}
        \DeclareTOCStyleEntry[beforeskip=6pt]{default}{section}
        \DeclareTOCStyleEntry[beforeskip=0pt]{default}{subsection}
        \makeatletter
        \def\@maketitle{
          {\LARGE \bfseries \@title \par}
                \vspace{1em}
                {\large \texttt{\href{https://www.linkedin.com/in/stells}{\@author}}, \authorrole, \texttt{\href{mailto:\authoremail}{\authoremail}} \par}
                \vspace{0.3em}
                {\normalsize \affiliationname \par}
                \vspace{2em}
        }
        \makeatother
  html: {}
generate-title-meta: false
metadata-files:
  - ./_include/author.founder.yml
  - ./_include/format.pdf.yml
  - ./_include/format.html.yml
---

ExaVerif[^ev] is a verification engine that exhaustively enumerates every valid combination of RISC‑V custom instruction fields, evaluates each deterministically, and records the result as a permanent, queryable Fact for neXus consumption. Think of it as a "hardware wallet for verification": the same cryptographic certainty that protects assets, now applied to custom silicon verification.

Its users range from verification engineers to design teams to autonomous verification agents. What they all share is a single source of truth—a continuous, auditable body of evidence that compounds across runs, tools, and time. This document outlines the product concept, market positioning, and technological foundation of ExaVerif, built on the open‑source neXus runtime[^nexus] for state‑space computation.

## Agentic verification base that never forgets

We are in the middle of 2026. RISC‑V has moved beyond early adoption—the technology market is projected to grow from $1.35B in 2025 to $10.7B by 2031 at a 41.2% CAGR. Custom instruction extensions (XIF) are its defining advantage, enabling domain‑specific acceleration for AI inference, cryptography, and embedded control. Every custom instruction must be verified across its full operational envelope.

The problem is not the tools. It is that every verification run starts from zero. Coverage is statistical or scoped to specific properties—no approach enumerates every valid constraint combination, evaluates each deterministically, and accumulates the result as reusable knowledge. The industry's answer has been complex infrastructure: random simulation, formal property checkers, cloud platforms. All produce ephemeral PASS/FAIL logs discarded at the end of each run.

ExaVerif does something fundamentally different. A YAML description becomes a Spec Space: field domains define its axes, constraints carve admissible subspaces. A single command enumerates and evaluates every point exhaustively. Every result is an immutable Fact—a permanent contribution to a shared body of verified knowledge that compounds across runs, across tools, across time. This is the beginning of exhaustive verification as a service: a category that didn't exist until now.

## Built on neXus

ExaVerif is the first verification product built on the neXus Runtime, an engine for state‑space computation. neXus provides foundational infrastructure: a model that structures knowledge, a time‑index for time‑travel queries, semantic search across verification space, and a contribution contract that ensures fairness in open networks.

The core engine is operational, validated against production RTL with exact agreement between static constraint evaluation and RISC‑V ISA simulation (Spike). What remains is integration and scalability—both proven paths made viable by the explosive growth of RISC‑V. ExaVerif's verification model is exhaustive rather than sampled. It treats verification as knowledge accumulation: each run expands the spec space, evaluates every point, and applies constraints. The result is not a PASS/FAIL log—it is permanent, queryable, auditable knowledge. That is the difference between verifying data and accumulating structured intelligence.

## The verification gap

The RISC‑V verification landscape in 2026 is well‑served by established tools. Open‑source frameworks like RISCV‑DV (Google) and TestRIG generate random instruction streams. RISC‑V ACT provides architectural conformance testing. The OpenHW CORE‑V‑VERIF suite offers a comprehensive UVM environment. Commercial offerings from Breker, Imperas, and Siemens (OneSpin) cover simulation and formal verification. The global EDA market reached $187.6B in 2026, with functional verification demand accelerating.

Yet a persistent gap remains. Random simulation delivers statistical coverage, not exhaustive proof. Formal property checking is powerful but requires deep expertise and is scoped to specific properties. The ACT suite cannot test custom instructions due to combinatorial explosion. Verification teams increasingly move to hybrid strategies combining simulation, formal, and hardware‑assisted methods—but no tool today provides complete, deterministic coverage of the entire constraint space.

This is the gap ExaVerif fills: a deterministic, exhaustive approach that systematically covers the space random and formal methods leave untouched. It is not a replacement—it is the missing piece in a hybrid verification strategy.

## Phased rollout

ExaVerif may launch in phases: from a CLI to validate the approach, to a full verification suite with ecosystem validation, and finally the ExaVerif Network—a shared knowledge store where verification results accumulate across teams and time. Every contribution enriches the whole. The business model is straightforward: subscription for advanced services, one‑time license for enterprise use, and optional upgrades for power users and teams. The timing is right—2026 is the year of verification automation, with major EDA vendors investing in AI‑assisted verification and custom silicon reaching mass adoption. ExaVerif is the first product to deliver exhaustive, deterministic verification of pre‑compiled instruction encoding in a portable form.

[^ev]: ExaVerif is an open‑core commercial‑extension project based on the open‑source neXus project.
[^nexus]: neXus is open source under Apache 2.0. Until the first production case like ExaVerif is publicly unveiled, the repository is kept invitation‑only by strategic choice.
